text: Legacy
Groundwork
Tipping point
Precedent
Turnaround
Entrenched
Status quo
In hindsight
Wake-up call
Uphill battle
I did not build that crisis. I inherited it, and then I gave it a name nobody had dared to use.
The attrition traced back to onboarding, a decision made two years before the resignations started.
The tension had been growing for months, and it came to a head the week our most senior teacher resigned.
Once the new onboarding gained momentum, engagement climbed on its own, and my job became protecting it.
We did not just slow the decline; we reversed the trend, and the exit interviews changed from warnings to thank-you notes.
Momentum
By the time the board reviewed the numbers, attrition had already fallen for two quarters.
When I inherited the team, engagement had been dropping for eighteen months, and nobody had traced it back to onboarding yet.
Long before the numbers moved, I had rebuilt onboarding and set up a quarterly pulse survey.
When it came to a head, the new system had already been running for a term, so the wake-up call caught us ready.
For two years before I arrived, attrition had been climbing and engagement had been falling, and nobody had traced it back to onboarding.
Long before the numbers moved, I had rebuilt onboarding, I had set up a quarterly pulse survey, and I had trained the leads on psychological safety.
Only then did engagement turn, and attrition followed it two quarters later. We did not slow the decline; we reversed the trend.
So what I am bringing to Canada is not the luck. It is the sequence, and the groundwork will have to be laid again from the beginning.

Before I take the credit you are about to test, let me give you the part that was not mine. When I inherited the Brazil team, engagement had been dropping for eighteen months, and nobody had traced it back to onboarding yet. I did not create that crisis. I named it.
A fair start. But naming a problem and fixing it are two different things, and the numbers recovered in a year when the whole sector recovered. So let me ask it plainly. How do you know it was you?

Because of the order. Long before the market moved, I had rebuilt onboarding, I had set up a quarterly pulse survey, and I had trained the leads. The groundwork had already been in place for two quarters when engagement turned, and engagement turned before attrition, and both turned before the sector did. Luck does not arrive in that sequence.
That is the first version of this story I have heard that has a middle. Tell me about the moment it came to a head. Every turnaround has one, and it is usually where the luck actually lives.

Our most senior teacher resigned, and in her exit interview she said what forty people had been thinking for a year. That was the wake-up call, and it was the tipping point, but it did not catch us unprepared, because the new onboarding had already been running for a term. The luck, if there was any, was that the crisis arrived after the groundwork, and not before it. And in hindsight, the one thing I would change is that I had not set the baseline earlier.

Then let us talk about Canada, because that is the only question left. You understand that the status quo there is entrenched in a culture you have not lived in, and that the groundwork will have to be laid again from the beginning. If you had told me the numbers turned because you arrived, I would have hesitated. You told me the sequence instead. That travels.
For two years before I arrived, attrition had been climbing, and nobody had yet traced it back to onboarding.
By the time the results appeared, the groundwork had already been in place for two quarters.
The tension had been building for months, and it came to a head the week our most senior teacher resigned.
We did not just slow the decline. We reversed the trend, and the exit interviews became thank-you notes.
In hindsight, the turnaround was a sequence, not an accident, and the sequence is what I am bringing to Canada.

Danielle, it is Claire. You review with the board tomorrow, and I want to tell you the one thing that will decide whether they hand you Canada or hesitate. They already know the Brazil numbers recovered. Attrition fell, engagement climbed, the exit interviews stopped reading like warnings. What they do not yet believe is that you caused it. And I understand why, because the way most people tell this story makes it sound like weather. They say, I arrived, and then things got better. Told like that, it is luck, and luck does not get sent to a country it has never worked in. So tell it as a sequence. By the time you were even asked to lead the Brazil turnaround, three things had already gone wrong, and they had been going wrong for two years. The attrition had been climbing since before your first day. The problem had traced back to onboarding long before anyone had put a name on it. And the moment it came to a head, the groundwork you had spent eight months laying was the only thing in the building that was ready. That is not weather, Danielle. That is a build. Make them hear the order of it, because the order is the proof.

Claire, it is Marcus Reid. You asked what I will actually be looking for tomorrow, so let me be plain, and you are welcome to tell her. I am not going to challenge the Brazil figures. I have read them, they are real, and they are good. What I do not yet know is whether they are repeatable, and that is a different question entirely. Every turnaround on paper looks like leadership after the fact, and half of them were a recovering market that would have lifted anyone. So the number that reassures me is not the one at the end. It is the sequence in the middle. If she can tell me what had already been failing before she touched it, and what she had put in place before the results appeared, then I am looking at cause, not coincidence. If all I hear is that she arrived and things improved, then with respect, I have funded that story before, and it did not survive contact with a second country. Canada is not Brazil. The status quo she is about to inherit is entrenched in a culture she has read about and not yet lived in. I want to know she understands that the groundwork has to be laid again, from the beginning, and that the last time, it took her the better part of a year before anything moved.

When I inherited the team, engagement had been dropping for eighteen months.
The problem traced back to onboarding, a decision made two years earlier.
Long before the results appeared, I had rebuilt onboarding and set up a quarterly pulse survey.
The tension had been building for months before it came to a head.
Once the new onboarding gained momentum, engagement climbed on its own.
For two years before I arrived, attrition had been climbing, and nobody had yet traced it back to onboarding.
By the time the results appeared, the groundwork had already been in place for two quarters.
The tension had been building for months, and it came to a head the week our most senior teacher resigned.
We did not just slow the decline. We reversed the trend, and the exit interviews became thank-you notes.
In hindsight, the turnaround was a sequence, not an accident, and the sequence is what I am bringing to Canada.
